# Derivation 3.4A — Stationary primitive source-to-field map {#sec-derivation-03-04A-source-field-map .unnumbered}

## Purpose

This appendix supports Chapter 3.4. It records the technical source-to-field map that the main text uses without making the chapter carry every convention and normalization detail.

The key point is that a primitive positive momentum-channel source has a clean stationary field response before any classical density, current, or metric dictionary is introduced.

## Role / regime / status

- **Role:** native M1 construction with stationary correspondence-ready normalization.
- **Regime:** stationary, weak-to-moderate field, exterior Green-kernel presentation.
- **Status:** reference derivation for the current gravity-chapter stationary field package.
- **Non-claim:** this is not a full microscopic source law and not a final nonstationary theory.

## Source channels and local arguments

For a chosen direction `k`, the primitive local source-density channels are

$$
\mathcal{J}_k^+(x),
\qquad
\mathcal{J}_k^-(x).
$$

The argument `x` marks the source location at which the channel density is defined. In the field integrals, the source point is written as `r'`, while the observation point is written as `r`.

In the stationary source normalization used here,

$$
[\mathcal{J}_k^\pm]=\mathrm{kg\,s^{-1}m^{-2}}.
$$

These are units of momentum content per spatial volume. The ordinary mass-density calibration is introduced later as correspondence language, not as the primitive source definition.

## Elementary directional contribution

Let

$$
R:=\|r-r'\|,
$$

and let $e_{(k)}^i(r')$ be the local orthonormal basis vector associated with direction `k` at the source point. With

$$
\kappa:=\frac{G_N}{c^3},
$$

a positive channel element contributes

$$
d\theta_i(r;r')
=
\kappa\,\frac{\mathcal{J}_k^+(r')}{\|r-r'\|}\,e_{(k)i}(r')\,d^3r'.
$$

The opposite channel contributes with the opposite directional sign,

$$
d\theta_i(r;r')
=
-\kappa\,\frac{\mathcal{J}_k^-(r')}{\|r-r'\|}\,e_{(k)i}(r')\,d^3r'.
$$

This is the elementary stationary source-to-field statement. The source is a positive directional momentum channel; the response is a directional field contribution with the same Green-kernel structure later used by the scalar package.

## Even and odd source packages

Define

$$
\mathcal{M}_k(x)=\frac{\mathcal{J}_k^+(x)+\mathcal{J}_k^-(x)}{2},
\qquad
\mathcal{P}_k(x)=\mathcal{J}_k^+(x)-\mathcal{J}_k^-(x).
$$

The even package $\mathcal{M}_k$ keeps what survives under directional sign reversal. The odd package $\mathcal{P}_k$ keeps the directional imbalance. These are local source-density packages, not integrated source totals.

The inverse bookkeeping is

$$
\mathcal{J}_k^\pm(x)=\mathcal{M}_k(x)\pm \tfrac12 \mathcal{P}_k(x).
$$

Under the carrier source map (Derivation 3.3A) the even package is axis-independent, $\mathcal{M}_k(x)=\mathcal{M}(x)=\varrho(x)$, the local core-momentum density; the scalar sector therefore has a single source field and no axis sum appears in the scalar map below.

## Projection lemma

Assume stationary linear response, opposite directional signs for the two senses of a channel, and the same Green kernel for the minimal scalar and directional response. With the stationary kernel

$$
\mathcal{K}[f](r):=\int_\Sigma\frac{f(r')}{\|r-r'\|}\,d^3r',
$$

linearity and the sign convention above imply that each channel pair can enter the field map only through

$$
\frac{\mathcal{J}_k^+(x)+\mathcal{J}_k^-(x)}{2},
\qquad
\mathcal{J}_k^+(x)-\mathcal{J}_k^-(x).
$$

The first combination is even under reversal of the channel sense. The second is odd. The carrier source map then identifies the even projection as axis-independent,

$$
\mathcal{M}_k(x)=\mathcal{M}(x),
$$

so the even response is one scalar field, not three independent scalar fields. The odd projections retain their directional labels and assemble into the vector-valued field:

$$
\theta_0(r)=\kappa\,\mathcal{K}[\mathcal{M}](r),
\qquad
\theta_i(r)=\kappa\sum_k\mathcal{K}[\mathcal{P}_k](r)e_{(k)i}.
$$

Thus the six ADMC source channels are not promoted to six deformation fields. In this stationary minimal-response map, they reduce by symmetry projection to one scalar depth $\theta_0$ plus three directional components $\theta_i$. This lemma records the projection structure assumed by the field map; it is not a microscopic derivation of the carrier source identification or of the depth-equals-stretch premise used later.

## Integrated stationary map

The scalar well is built from the even package:

$$
\theta_0(r)
=
\kappa\int_\Sigma
\frac{\mathcal{M}(r')}{\|r-r'\|}\,d^3r'.
$$

The directional field is built from the odd package:

$$
\theta_i(r)
=
\kappa\sum_k\int_\Sigma
\frac{\mathcal{P}_k(r')}{\|r-r'\|}\,e_{(k)i}(r')\,d^3r'.
$$

Equivalently, directly in the primitive channels,

$$
\theta_0(r)
=
\frac{G_N}{2c^3}\int_\Sigma
\frac{\mathcal{J}_k^+(r')+\mathcal{J}_k^-(r')}{\|r-r'\|}\,d^3r'
\quad\text{(any fixed }k\text{)},
$$

and

$$
\theta_i(r)
=
\frac{G_N}{c^3}\sum_k\int_\Sigma
\frac{\mathcal{J}_k^+(r')-\mathcal{J}_k^-(r')}{\|r-r'\|}\,e_{(k)i}(r')\,d^3r'.
$$

Thus the field map preserves the source grammar: addition feeds scalar depth; directional imbalance feeds shift-like structure.

## Differential form

Using

$$
\Delta\left(\frac{1}{R}\right)=-4\pi\delta^{(3)}(r-r'),
$$

the integral equations imply

$$
\Delta\theta_0(r)=-4\pi\kappa\,\mathcal{M}(r),
$$

and

$$
\Delta\theta_i(r)=-4\pi\kappa\sum_k\mathcal{P}_k(r)e_{(k)i}(r).
$$

In primitive-channel form,

$$
\Delta\theta_0(r)
=-\frac{2\pi G_N}{c^3}\left(\mathcal{J}_k^+(r)+\mathcal{J}_k^-(r)\right)
\quad\text{(any fixed }k\text{)},
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_i(r)
=-\frac{4\pi G_N}{c^3}\sum_k\left(\mathcal{J}_k^+(r)-\mathcal{J}_k^-(r)\right)e_{(k)i}(r).
$$

## Momentum-transport note

The minimal stationary map above uses the zeroth and first source moments, $\mathcal{M}$ and $\mathcal{P}_i$, as independent field inputs. Full carrier-level momentum accounting also contains the second transport moment

$$
\mathcal{C}_{ij}(x)
:=
\left\langle
\sum_a \frac{p_{i,a}p_{j,a}}{M_a}
\right\rangle_x,
\qquad
M_a=\frac{E_a}{c}.
$$

This object appears natively once one asks for local conservation of the directional momentum package. Schematically, in a local flat bookkeeping frame and with $t_*$ the bookkeeping time used for the local transport balance,

$$
\partial_{t_*}\mathcal{P}_i
+ c\,\partial_j\mathcal{C}_{ij}
= f_i .
$$

The factor $c\,\mathcal{C}_{ij}$ is the flux of $\mathcal{P}_i$: it records how momentum component $i$ is transported through direction $j$. Thus $\mathcal{C}_{ij}$ is not imported from the GR stress tensor. It is the second moment required by the same carrier bookkeeping whenever pressure, counterflow, shear, anisotropic dispersion, radiation, or internal transport must be represented. The present appendix neglects independent $\mathcal{C}_{ij}$ field contributions only to keep the stationary scalar-and-shift construction minimal.

Here $f_i$ denotes whatever external force-density or source-sink term is present in that local balance. The equation is a transport-accounting schematic, not a completed field-response law for $\mathcal{C}_{ij}$.

## Result

The stationary field route is

$$
\mathcal{J}_k^\pm
\longrightarrow
(\mathcal{M}_k,\mathcal{P}_k)
\longrightarrow
(\theta_0,\theta_i)
\longrightarrow
(G,A_i,B_{ij}).
$$

In the packaging step, the scalar sector enters through $G=\sqrt{1-2\theta_0}$ and the directional sector through $A_i=-\kappa_A\,\theta_i^\perp$, where $\kappa_A$ is the dimensionless coefficient of the GR-facing packaging. Its value ($\kappa_A=4$ in current conventions) is derived by the chain summarized in Chapter 3.6 (Derivations 3.3A, 3.4B, 3.6C) and validated against the frame-drag endpoint in Derivation 3.6B; nothing in the source-to-field map itself depends on it.

The important structural result is not merely that M1 can reproduce familiar variables later. It is that the primitive momentum-channel source already maps cleanly into the scalar and directional field sectors before the GR-facing dictionary is applied.
